note.soap:
S:
Patient reports tick bite. Reports a tick bite with a bullseye rash present. No systemic symptoms reported. Exposure occurred in Georgia.
O: No physical exam available due to phone-only encounter.

A:  
  - Working diagnosis (most likely): Early localized Lyme disease presenting with erythema migrans (bullseye rash).  
  - Differential:  
    1. Early Lyme disease  
    2. Southern tick-associated rash illness (STARI)  
    3. Cellulitis or local skin infection  
    4. Allergic reaction to tick bite  
  - Red flags considered: No systemic symptoms such as fever, severe headache, neck stiffness, or neurological deficits reported.

P:  
  - Disposition: Manage in urgent care setting with outpatient treatment; no immediate emergency referral required given absence of systemic symptoms or red flags.  
  - Plan: Initiate doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 14 days as first-line treatment for early Lyme disease, given the presence of erythema migrans and tick exposure in an endemic area. If pregnancy status is confirmed or suspected, consider amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily for 14 days instead. Educate patient on completing the full antibiotic course and monitoring for any new or worsening symptoms.  
  - Safety net: Advise patient to seek urgent care or emergency evaluation if fever, worsening rash, joint pain, neurological symptoms, or other systemic signs develop. Recommend follow-up with primary care provider within 1 week to reassess clinical status and confirm pregnancy status if relevant.

Evidence (snippets used): None available; diagnosis based on clinical presentation of bullseye rash after tick attachment in Georgia.
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.

evidence.title: CDC: What to Do After a Tick Bite
CDC: Guidance for Clinicians—Caring for Patients after a Tick Bite (PDF)
IDSA/AAN/ACR: 2020 Guidelines for Prevention, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Lyme Disease
AAFP: Lyme Disease—Updated Recommendations from IDSA/AAN/ACR (2021)
